serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried battered fish fillet coated in garlic-black-pepper sauce alongside a mound of steamed white rice, as typically served in a Singaporean food-court or GrabFood delivery context.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (composite estimate: fried fish with rice)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for garlic-black-pepper crispy fish with steamed white rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against HPB Foodo entries for fried fish (battered, deep-fried) and steamed white rice. The input per-100g values are internally consistent with a mixed dish of approximately 35% fried fish and 65% steamed rice by weight. Values are retained with medium confidence pending a direct HPB or USDA FoodData Central composite match. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for 'Fish, battered or breaded, fried' (NDB 15196) and 'Rice, white, cooked' (NDB 20444) were used as secondary cross-checks to validate plausibility of the macro profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, steamed (analogous anchor)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being steamed white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) diluted by the fried fish component (negligible carbohydrate GI contribution) and the batter coating (moderate GI). The composite dish GI is estimated conservatively at 65 (medium band). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 60.0 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 = 39.0. Carbohydrate per serving = 13.33 g/100g × 4.5 = 59.99 g ≈ 60.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 333 mg/100 g (1500 mg per 450 g serving) from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning salt, with insufficient potassium offset and deep-fried preparation adding cardiovascular risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request reduced soy and oyster sauce, and substitute steamed or grilled fish for the deep-fried preparation to reduce sodium and saturated-fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined-carbohydrate load from steamed white rice (dominant component) and wheat-flour batter on the fish, with low dietary fibre (0.67 g/100 g) producing a medium-to-high glycaemic load of approximately 39 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the white rice portion, substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice where possible, and choose steamed or grilled fish without batter to lower the glycaemic load and postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and elevates total fat (8.89 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.11 g/100 g); combined with high sodium (333 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre, this profile is unfavourable for cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the deep-fried preparation and request steamed or baked fish, reduce sauce volume to lower sodium, and pair with a vegetable side to increase fibre and potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs that override the potential omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit of the fish fillet; wheat-flour batter adds refined carbohydrate and gluten.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request steamed or grilled fish to preserve omega-3 benefits and eliminate AGE formation, and monitor tolerance to nightshade garnishes such as bell pepper.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Black Pepper Crispy Fish with Steamed White Rice is a Singaporean food-court and delivery-platform dish with Thai culinary influences, consisting of a white fish fillet (typically dory or basa) coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a bold stir-fried sauce of minced garlic, cracked black pepper, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, served alongside a generous portion of steamed jasmine white rice. The dish delivers a satisfying contrast of textures — a crunchy, savoury fish exterior against soft, fluffy rice — with the pungent heat of black pepper and the aromatic depth of garlic as the dominant flavour notes. Per 100 g the dish provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 8.9 g fat, and 13.3 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 333 mg reflecting the soy-sauce and oyster-sauce seasoning. The glycaemic load per standard 450 g plate is estimated at approximately 39, placing it in the high glycaemic-load range for a single meal. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high refined-carbohydrate load, low fibre), or cardiovascular disease (deep-fried preparation, trans fat, high sodium). The deep-frying method also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making it a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Garlic and black pepper contribute bioactive compounds (allicin, piperine) with anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are largely offset by the frying method. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean food-court contexts and contains fish, gluten, soy, and shellfish (oyster sauce) allergens.

notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite fried-fish-and-rice dish are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a single composite entry as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient panel data for this composite dish are not available in a single approved-source composite entry; HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy were consulted and do not report these values for an equivalent composite dish. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.11 g/100 g from the input record; this is plausible for a deep-fried product using partially hydrogenated or commercial frying oil and is retained. 3. The dish is listed under food_group 'Fish and seafood' consistent with the input record, though the dominant caloric contributor by weight is steamed white rice; this classification reflects the dish's identity as a fish-centred plate. 4. Cuisine is classified as Thai-Singaporean fusion: the dish is sold in Singapore via GrabFood and the cooking style (garlic-black-pepper stir-fry sauce) reflects Thai culinary influence, consistent with the input cultural tags. 5. Multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi (mandatory for Singaporean dishes), and Thai (mandatory per Thai cuisine tag). 6. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
